title: Improved Cryogenic Photodiode Optical Biasing for Low-Noise and Low-Jitter Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors
authors: Jia-Hao Hu, Wei-Jun Zhang, Wen-Shuo Yu, Yu-Ze Wang, Dong-Wei Chu, Ya-Tao Peng, Hui-Qin Yu, Pu-Sheng Yuan, Ling Wu, Li-Xing You
abstract: We experimentally demonstrate an improved optical biasing scheme for superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs), which employs a cryogenic InGaAs-InP photodiode (PD) as a local bias source. It is found that, under illumination from a stable external light source, this PD generates a stable photocurrent in a cryogenic environment (~2.3 K), with fluctuations in the photocurrent primarily attributed to fluctuations in the incident optical power. Furthermore, by screening and effectively blocking stray photons leaking from the PD, which give rise to background dark counts, we have achieved an SNSPD exhibiting an ultra-low intrinsic dark count rate of 1e-4 cps. Utilizing this improved optical biasing technique, our SNSPD achieved performance comparable to that obtained under conventional electrical biasing: a system detection efficiency of 80.7%, a background dark count rate of 32.6 cps, and a minimum timing jitter of 57.5 ps. These results indicate that cryogenic-PD-based optical biasing serves as a viable, low-noise, and low-jitter alternative to traditional electrical biasing. Moreover, this work offers useful design guidance for the future development of PD-based low-noise bias sources and for the construction of all-photonic SNSPD systems tailored for high-precision quantum photonics applications.
summary: The paper presents an improved optical biasing technique for SNSPDs using a cryogenic photodiode as the local current source. By effectively shielding stray photons, they achieved ultra-low dark count rates while maintaining high detection efficiency and low jitter compared to electrical methods. This establishes PD-based optical biasing as a viable path toward building all-photonic quantum photonics systems.
main_problem: Achieving low-noise and low-jitter single-photon detection is critical for quantum information processing, as conventional electrical biasing introduces noise and thermal load.
main_result: The authors demonstrated that using a cryogenic photodiode (PD) for optical biasing provides an alternative to electrical methods, achieving performance comparable in efficiency and jitter while maintaining ultra-low intrinsic dark count rates after proper shielding.
method: They experimentally compared the performance metrics of Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors (SNSPDs) biased optically versus electrically, focusing on noise suppression techniques.
model_or_system: The core system is an SNSPD operating at cryogenic temperatures (~2.3 K). The novel element is using a local InGaAs-InP photodiode as a stable optical bias source to replace traditional electrical biasing circuits.
key_observables: System Detection Efficiency (80.7%), background dark count rate (32.6 cps, intrinsic DCR of $1 	imes 10^{-4}$ cps), and minimum timing jitter (57.5 ps).
important_parameters: Cryogenic temperature ($\sim 2.3$ K); stability metrics like Coefficient of Variation (CV) for photocurrent fluctuations.
main_assumptions: The primary limitation identified is that residual optical power fluctuations remain a dominant noise source, necessitating ultra-stable light sources and improved coupling methods.
figures_summary: Figures compare the SNSPD performance ($	ext{SDE}$, $	ext{DCR}$, jitter) under optical versus electrical biasing across different operating points, and illustrate how stray photon leakage from connectors increases background counts.
paper_structure: The paper follows a structure of identifying the noise limitations of existing bias methods, proposing the PD-based optical biasing scheme, demonstrating its stability and low dark count rate through shielding, and finally comparing its achieved performance metrics against conventional electrical biasing.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses hardware requirements for quantum photonics by providing a scalable, low-noise method to bias superconducting detectors essential for quantum communication and computing applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
